DISCOVERY, 302, PLAINTIFF ROBERT GRICE'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES TO FORM INTERROGATORIES GENERAL, SET ONE AND FURTHER PRODUCTION AND FOR SANCTIONS *AS TO THE ISSUE OF THE MOVING PARTIE'S SANCTIONS ONLY* IS SUBMITTED ON MAR-13-2018 BY PRO TEM STEVEN STEIN. - PLAINTIFF ROBERT GRICE'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES TO FORM INTERROGATORIES GENERAL, SET ONE AND FURTHER PRODUCTION AND FOR SANCTIONS IS GRANTED. THE ISSUE OF SANCTIONS REQUESTED BY THE MOVING PARTY IS TAKEN UNDER SUBMISSION. SANCTIONS REQUESTED BY THE OPPOSING PARTY ARE DENIED. PLAINTIFF'S COUNSEL IS TO PREPARE A FORM OF ORDER IN COMPLIANCE WITH CRC 3.1312 AND SUBMIT IT TO JUDGE PRO TEM STEVEN STEIN. DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-26-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Atty. Ian Anderson of the Kaufman, Dolowich & Voluck LLP, Tel. No. 1-415-926-7614
appearing for the Plaintiff;
Atty. Matthew K. Telford of Hopkins Carley, Tel. No. 1-408-286-9800 appearing
for the Defendant Edward Cunningham;
Atty. Brish Miller of the Roberts & Elliot LLP, Tel. No. 1-408-275-9800 appearing
for the Defendant Praktek Corporation by telephone appearance.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. Defendant Edward Cunningham's demurrer to the third cause of action for breach of fiduciary duty in the first amended complaint filed by plaintiff Robert Grice is overruled. Jara v. Suprema Meats (2004) 121 Cal. App. 4th 1238, a decision of the First District which has not been overruled or criticized by any published authority, holds that a claim brought by a minority shareholder alleging that payments made by a corporation to a majority shareholder were in the form of excessive compensation to the majority shareholder and can be the basis of an individual claim on behalf of the minority shareholder against the majority shareholder. Liberally construed, the third cause of action alleges that Praktek Corporation made payments to Mr. Cunningham or entities controlled by Mr. Cunningham in the form of excessive compensation to Mr. Cunningham and thus Jara permits Mr. Grice to sue on his own behalf based on those payments. The fact that there are two other shareholders in Praktek who hold a small percentage interest in the company does not warrant a different result under the reasoning in Jara. The two cases primarily relied on by Mr. Cunningham predate Jara and are distinguished in Jara as involving claims "dealing with mismanagement ' or fraud." The third cause of action does not allege either mismanagement or fraud by Mr. Cunningham, so those cases (Nelson and PacLink) are inapplicable. Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR # 8600 =302/hk

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or Alternatively Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Mar-12-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT EDWARD CUNNINGHAM's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or Alternatively Summary Adjudication.

Defendant Edward Cunningham's motion for summary judgment is denied and his alternate motion for summary adjudication is granted with ten days leave to amend as to the first cause of action for involuntary dissolution and denied as to the second cause of action for removal of director. As to the first cause of action, the motion for summary adjudication is treated as a motion for judgment on the pleadings. (C.L. Smith Co. v. Roger Ducharme, Inc. (1977) 65 Cal.App.3d 735, 745.) Mr. Cunningham correctly cites Weisman v. Odell (1970) 3 Cal.App.3d 494, 498 for the proposition that he cannot be compelled to be a party to a dissolution action against his will. Per plaintiff Robert Grice's request for leave to amend, Mr. Grice is given leave to amend to re-style the claim as a cause of action for breach of fiduciary duty against Mr. Cunningham. As to the second cause of action, while Mr. Cunningham correctly cites the "general" rule that a reelected director will not be removed for conduct that occurred during the previous term, that general rule is not applicable where the director acted illegally in obtaining his new term and otherwise acted his own self-interest and against the interests of the corporation. Per Corporations Code 305, the court has broad discretion to remove a director. There is evidence that Mr. Cunningham called a shareholder's meeting without providing notice to Mr. Grice, violated Corporations 310(a) by entering into self-interested transactions, and used corporate funds to pay for personal counsel.

Counsel for Mr. Grice is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CCP 437c(g) and CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, torreanom@aol.com, Reported. (D302/HK)
